Key Takeaway: Slicing returns a VIEW — modifying it modifies the original. Always use .copy() when you want independence. Boolean indexing is faster than loops for filtering large arrays.

blogs.title: NumPy Mathematics: Vectorized Ops, Statistics, and Linear Algebra
blogs.description: A complete guide to NumPy's mathematical capabilities — element-wise operations, trigonometric functions, broadcasting rules, statistics, and linear algebra with np.linalg.
blogs.author: NumPy Notes
blogs.content:
Element-Wise Mathematics

NumPy operations apply to every element without writing a loop. This is called vectorization.

a = np.array([2, 4, 6])

a + 2   # [4, 6, 8]
a * 2   # [4, 8, 12]
a ** 2  # [4, 16, 36]
a / 2   # [1., 2., 3.]

b = np.array([1, 2, 3])
a + b   # Element-wise: [3, 6, 9]

Trigonometric and Math Functions

np.sin(a)
np.cos(a)
np.sqrt(a)
np.log(a)     # Natural log
np.exp(a)     # e^x

Broadcasting — The Power Feature

Broadcasting lets NumPy perform operations on arrays of different shapes without copying data. The rules work from the trailing (rightmost) dimensions.

a = np.array([1, 2, 3])       # Shape: (3,)
b = np.array([[10], [20]])    # Shape: (2, 1)

a + b
# Result shape: (2, 3)
# [[11, 12, 13],
#  [21, 22, 23]]

Broadcasting Rules: Two dimensions are compatible if they are equal, or one of them is 1. If one is 1, it gets stretched to match.

Statistics

mat = np.random.randint(1, 100, size=(4, 6))

np.min(mat)           # Global minimum
np.max(mat, axis=0)   # Column-wise maximum
np.min(mat, axis=1)   # Row-wise minimum
np.sum(mat, axis=0)   # Column-wise sum

np.mean(mat)
np.median(mat)
np.std(mat)           # Standard deviation
np.var(mat)           # Variance
np.argmax(mat)        # Index of maximum value
np.argmin(mat, axis=1) # Row-wise index of minimum

Linear Algebra

from numpy import linalg

a = np.ones((2, 3))
b = np.full((3, 4), 4)

# Matrix multiplication (shapes must be compatible)
np.matmul(a, b)  # (2x3) @ (3x4) = (2x4)
a @ b            # Same, cleaner syntax

# Determinant (square matrices only)
np.linalg.det(np.identity(3))

# Other linalg functions
np.linalg.inv(A)          # Matrix inverse
np.linalg.solve(A, b)     # Solve Ax = b
np.linalg.eig(A)          # Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
np.linalg.norm(A)         # Matrix norm
np.linalg.matrix_rank(A)  # Rank

Handling NaN and Infinity

a = np.array([1.0, np.nan, 3.0, np.inf])

np.isnan(a)      # [False, True, False, False]
np.isinf(a)      # [False, False, False, True]
np.nanmean(a)    # Mean ignoring NaN
np.nansum(a)     # Sum ignoring NaN
np.nan_to_num(a, nan=0.0, posinf=1e9)

Key Takeaway: Broadcasting is one of NumPy's most powerful features — understanding the rules saves you from many reshape operations. Use nanmean/nansum to safely aggregate arrays with missing values.

blogs.title: NumPy Advanced: np.where, Boolean Ops, Sorting, and Searching
blogs.description: Master NumPy's filtering and searching toolkit — np.where for vectorized conditionals, np.select for multi-condition logic, np.take, sorting with argsort, and useful utility functions.
blogs.author: NumPy Notes
blogs.content:
np.where — Vectorized IF-ELSE

np.where is the NumPy equivalent of an if-else statement, applied element-wise across an entire array.

arr = np.array([10, 5, 30, 3])

# Mode 1: Select values based on condition
np.where(arr > 10, "High", "Low")
# ['Low', 'Low', 'High', 'Low']

# Mode 2: Get indices where condition is True
np.where(arr > 10)  # (array([2]),) — index 2 satisfies

# Mode 3: Clamp values
arr2 = np.where(arr < 10, arr, 10)  # Cap at 10
# [10, 5, 10, 3]

np.any() and np.all()

arr = np.array([0, 0, 5, 0])
np.any(arr > 0)     # True — at least one is > 0

arr2 = np.array([2, 4, 6])
np.all(arr2 % 2 == 0)  # True — all are even

# Axis-wise
mat = np.array([[0, 0, 1], [2, 3, 4]])
np.any(mat > 0, axis=1)  # [True, True]
np.all(mat > 0, axis=1)  # [False, True]

np.select — Multiple Conditions

arr = np.array([5, 12, 25, 40, 7])

conditions = [arr < 10, (arr >= 10) & (arr < 30), arr >= 30]
choices = ["Low", "Medium", "High"]

np.select(conditions, choices, default="Unknown")
# ['Low', 'Medium', 'Medium', 'High', 'Low']

np.take — Fast Index Selection

arr = np.array([10, 20, 30, 40])
np.take(arr, [1, 3])  # [20, 40]

# Works on 2D with axis
np.take(mat, [0, 2], axis=1)  # Columns 0 and 2

Sorting

a = np.array([3, 1, 4, 1, 5])
np.sort(a)        # Returns sorted copy
a.sort()          # In-place sort

# argsort — get indices that would sort the array
np.argsort(a)     # Very useful for custom ordering
a[np.argsort(a)]  # Same as sorted

# Sort 2D rows by a column
order = np.argsort(mat[:, 0])  # Sort by first column
mat[order]

Other Useful Functions

np.clip(arr, 0, 100)           # Limit values to range
np.unique(arr, return_counts=True)  # Unique + frequencies
np.argmax(arr)                 # Index of maximum
np.nonzero(arr)                # Indices of non-zero elements
np.argwhere(arr > 10)          # Coordinates where True

Key Takeaway: np.where is the vectorized replacement for Python if-else loops. np.select handles multiple conditions like an if-elif-else chain. Use argsort when you need the ordering, not just the sorted values.

blogs.title: Pandas Series and DataFrame: The Foundation of Data Analysis
blogs.description: A complete introduction to Pandas — what Series and DataFrame are, how they are created, their key properties, and the conceptual difference between Pandas and NumPy.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
What is Pandas?

Pandas is a Python library built on NumPy that provides two primary data structures for tabular data analysis: Series (1D) and DataFrame (2D). It is the standard tool for data loading, cleaning, transformation, and exploration in Python.

Series — Labeled 1D Array

A Series is a 1-dimensional labeled array that can hold any data type. Think of it as a single column from a spreadsheet.

import pandas as pd

# Creating Series
s = pd.Series([10, 20, 30])                    # Auto index
s = pd.Series([10, 20, 30], index=['a','b','c'])  # Custom index
s = pd.Series({'a': 10, 'b': 20})              # From dict

# Properties
s.values    # Underlying NumPy array
s.index     # Index labels
s.dtype     # Data type
s.name = "marks"

DataFrame — Labeled 2D Table

A DataFrame is a 2D labeled data structure with rows and columns. Each column is a Series. Think of it as an Excel sheet, a SQL table, or a dict of Series.

# From dict (most common)
data = {"name": ["Alice", "Bob"], "age": [25, 30]}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# From list of dicts (API/JSON pattern)
data = [{"a": 1, "b": 2}, {"a": 3, "b": 4}]
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# From NumPy array
df = pd.DataFrame(np.ones((3, 3)), columns=['a','b','c'])

# From zip
names = ['Tom', 'Jane']
ages = [25, 30]
df = pd.DataFrame(list(zip(names, ages)), columns=["name", "age"])

DataFrame Properties

df.shape      # (rows, cols)
df.ndim       # 2
df.dtypes     # Data type per column
df.columns    # Column labels
df.index      # Row labels
df.info()     # Full summary
df.describe() # Statistical summary of numeric columns

Series vs DataFrame

FeatureSeriesDataFrame
Dimensions1D2D
StructureSingle columnTable
Data typesOne dtypeMultiple dtypes
IndexRow index onlyRow + column labels

Key Takeaway: df["col"] returns a Series. df[["col"]] returns a DataFrame. This distinction matters when you chain operations.

blogs.title: Pandas Data Selection: loc, iloc, Boolean Filtering, and query()
blogs.description: Master all methods to select data from a Pandas DataFrame — column selection, loc for label-based access, iloc for positional access, boolean masks, and the powerful query() method.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
Column Selection

# Single column — returns Series
df['Name']

# Multiple columns — returns DataFrame
df[['Name', 'Age']]

# Columns by position range
df[df.columns[1:4]]

loc — Label-Based Selection

loc uses row/column labels. The end index is inclusive.

df.loc[0, 'Name']         # Single value
df.loc[0:2, 'A':'C']      # Row 0-2, columns A-C (inclusive)
df.loc[[0,1,2], ['A','C']] # Specific rows and columns

# Condition + select columns (best practice)
df.loc[df['Age'] > 25, ['Name', 'Age']]

iloc — Position-Based Selection

iloc uses integer positions. The end index is exclusive (like Python slicing).

df.iloc[0]           # First row
df.iloc[[4,5,6]]     # Rows 4, 5, 6
df.iloc[0, 1]        # Row 0, column 1
df.iloc[0:3, 1:3]    # Slice by position

Boolean Indexing — The Right Way

Pandas filtering is based on boolean masks — a Series of True/False values.

# Single condition
df[df['Salary'] >= 50000]

# Multiple conditions — use & | ~ NOT and/or/not
df[(df['Age'] < 40) & (df['Salary'] > 50000)]
df[(df['Age'] < 25) | (df['Dept'] == 'IT')]
df[~(df['Dept'] == 'IT')]  # NOT

# Always use parentheses around each condition!

Useful Filtering Methods

df[df['City'].isin(['NY', 'LA'])]    # In a list
df[df['Age'].between(25, 40)]        # Range
df[df['Name'].str.startswith('A')]   # String pattern
df[df['Salary'].notna()]             # Non-null values

query() — SQL-Like Filtering

df.query("Age < 40 & Salary > 50000 & Dept == 'IT'")

# Using Python variables with @
min_salary = 50000
df.query("Salary > @min_salary")

# IN operator
df.query("Dept in ['IT', 'HR']")

Copy vs View Warning

# Wrong — may cause SettingWithCopyWarning
filtered = df[df['Age'] > 25]
filtered['Age'] = 0  # Warning!

# Correct
filtered = df.loc[df['Age'] > 25].copy()
filtered['Age'] = 0  # Safe

Key Takeaway: Use loc for label-based access, iloc for positional. Always use & | ~ for multiple conditions, never Python's and/or/not. Always .copy() when creating a filtered subset you plan to modify.

blogs.title: Handling Missing Data in Pandas: Detection, Filling, and Dropping
blogs.description: A complete guide to missing data in Pandas — detecting NaN values, filling with constants or statistics, forward/backward fill, interpolation, and when to drop rows or columns.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
What is Missing Data?

Pandas represents missing data as NaN (Not a Number) for numeric types, None for object types, and NaT for datetime types. Missing data occurs from data entry errors, sensor failures, optional fields, and data integration issues.

Detecting Missing Values

df.isnull()            # True where missing
df.isna()              # Same as isnull
df.notnull()           # True where present

df.isnull().sum()      # Count per column
df.isnull().mean() * 100  # % missing per column

# Filter rows with missing values
df[df['Gender'].isnull()]
df[df['Gender'].notnull()]

Filling Missing Values

# Fill all with zero
df.fillna(0)

# Column-wise with statistics (best practice)
df['Age'].fillna(df['Age'].mean(), inplace=True)
df['Salary'].fillna(df['Salary'].median(), inplace=True)
df['Category'].fillna(df['Category'].mode()[0], inplace=True)

# Column-wise with dict
df.fillna({'salary': df['salary'].mean(), 'age': 0})

# Forward fill (use previous value)
df.ffill()

# Backward fill (use next value)
df.bfill()

# With limit (only fill N consecutive NaNs)
df.ffill(limit=2)

replace() vs fillna()

# replace() is for any value, not just NaN
df.replace(np.nan, -999)      # Sentinel value
df.replace("N/A", np.nan)     # Clean string placeholders

Interpolation — Smart Filling for Time Series

df.interpolate(method='linear')                  # Basic linear
df.interpolate(method='linear', limit_direction='both')  # Fill both directions

Use interpolation for sensor data, stock prices, and other time series — never for categorical data or IDs.

Dropping Missing Values

df.dropna()                    # Drop rows with any NaN
df.dropna(how='all')           # Drop rows where ALL values are NaN
df.dropna(axis=1)              # Drop columns with NaN
df.dropna(thresh=3)            # Keep rows with at least 3 non-null values
df.dropna(subset=['Salary'])   # Drop only if Salary is NaN

Common Mistakes

  • Comparing with NaN directly: df[df['Age'] == np.nan] — always returns empty. Use isnull().
  • Using ffill/bfill on salary or financial columns without business justification
  • Dropping rows without first checking what percentage is missing

Key Takeaway: Always inspect missing data with isnull().sum() before deciding strategy. Fill with statistics for numeric columns, use mode for categoricals. Only drop if the percentage is small or all values in a row are missing.

blogs.title: Pandas groupby: Split-Apply-Combine Explained
blogs.description: Master Pandas groupby — the split-apply-combine pattern, aggregation with agg(), transformation with transform(), group filtering, and all key parameters explained.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
The Mental Model: Split → Apply → Combine

Every groupby operation follows this pattern: Split the DataFrame into groups, Apply a function to each group, and Combine the results. This replaces manual loops and filters with a single expression.

groupby() Syntax and Parameters

df.groupby(
    by=None,           # Column(s) to group by
    as_index=True,     # Group keys as index (False = as columns)
    sort=True,         # Sort group keys
    dropna=True        # Exclude NaN groups
)

Basic Grouping

# Group by single column
gender_groups = df.groupby('Gender')

# Iterate over groups
for name, group in gender_groups:
    print(name, group['Salary'].count())

# Group by multiple columns
team_gender = df.groupby(['Gender', 'Team'])

Aggregation with agg()

# Single aggregation
df.groupby('Team')['Salary'].mean()

# Multiple aggregations on same column
df.groupby('Team').agg(
    min_salary=('Salary', 'min'),
    max_salary=('Salary', 'max'),
    avg_salary=('Salary', 'mean'),
    emp_count=('Team', 'count')
)

# Multiple columns, multiple functions
df.groupby('Dept').agg({
    'Salary': ['mean', 'max'],
    'Age': 'median'
})

transform() — Same-Shape Output

Unlike aggregation which reduces rows, transform() returns results aligned to the original DataFrame — essential for adding group-level statistics back as columns.

# Rank each employee within their team
df['Rank_in_Team'] = df.groupby('Team')['Salary'].transform(
    lambda x: x.rank(ascending=False)
)

# Z-score within group
df['salary_zscore'] = df.groupby('Team')['Salary'].transform(
    lambda x: (x - x.mean()) / x.std()
)

# Fill NaN with group mean
df['Salary'] = df.groupby('Team')['Salary'].transform(
    lambda x: x.fillna(x.mean())
)

filter() — Keep or Drop Entire Groups

# Keep only teams where mean salary >= 1M
high_pay_teams = df.groupby('Team').filter(
    lambda x: x['Salary'].mean() >= 1_000_000
)

size() vs count()

df.groupby('Team').size()           # Total rows per group (includes NaN)
df.groupby('Team')['Age'].count()   # Non-null values per group

Key Takeaway: Use agg() for summaries, transform() when you need results at the original row level, filter() to include/exclude whole groups. Use as_index=False for cleaner output when exporting.

blogs.title: Pandas Combining DataFrames: concat, merge, and join
blogs.description: When to use concat vs merge vs join in Pandas — understanding the mental model, all merge types (inner, left, right, outer), and the correct approach for each real-world scenario.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
The Decision Model

MethodUse WhenSQL Equivalent
concat()Same structure, stack or alignUNION
merge()Match rows by column valuesJOIN
join()Match rows by indexIndex-based JOIN

pd.concat() — Stacking Data

pd.concat(
    objs,               # List of DataFrames
    axis=0,             # 0=stack rows, 1=stack columns
    join='outer',       # 'outer'=union, 'inner'=intersection
    ignore_index=False, # Reset index after concat
    keys=None           # Add hierarchical index
)

# Vertical stacking
result = pd.concat([df1, df2], ignore_index=True)

# Horizontal stacking
pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=1)

# Track source
pd.concat([df1, df2], keys=['train', 'test'])

pd.merge() — SQL-Style Joins

pd.merge(
    left, right,
    how='inner',       # 'inner','left','right','outer'
    on=None,           # Column(s) in both DataFrames
    left_on=None,      # Column in left (when names differ)
    right_on=None,     # Column in right
    suffixes=('_x','_y')  # Handle overlapping column names
)

# Simple inner join
pd.merge(orders, customers, on='customer_id')

# Left join (keep all left rows)
pd.merge(orders, customers, how='left', on='id')

# Merge on different column names
pd.merge(df1, df2,
         left_on='acc_number',
         right_on='account_NO',
         how='inner')

# Multi-key merge
pd.merge(df1, df2, on=['date', 'store_id'])

Understanding join types

  • inner: Only matching keys — intersection
  • left: All left rows, NaN for unmatched right
  • right: All right rows, NaN for unmatched left
  • outer: All rows from both, NaN where no match

DataFrame.join() — Index-Based

# Default: joins on index
df1.join(df2, how='left', lsuffix='_l', rsuffix='_r')

# Join using column from left to index of right
df1.join(df2, on='key', how='inner')

Common Mistakes

  • Using concat when data has different schemas — check column names first
  • Forgetting suffixes when column names overlap
  • Using join() with multiple key columns — use merge() instead
  • Not checking for duplicate keys before merge (can cause row explosion)

Key Takeaway: concat for stacking same-structure tables, merge for relational joins on column values (covers 90% of join use cases), join for index-based alignment. Always check key uniqueness before merging.

blogs.title: Pandas pivot() and pivot_table(): Reshaping Data for Analysis
blogs.description: Understand when and how to use Pandas pivot and pivot_table — the difference between them, all parameters explained, multiple aggregation functions, and real-world use cases.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
Why Pivoting Exists

Real data is usually in long format (one row per observation). Analysts often need it in wide format (one row per entity, different conditions as columns). Pivoting transforms between these forms.

pivot() — Pure Reshape, No Aggregation

df.pivot(
    index='Category',      # What becomes rows
    columns='Product',     # What becomes columns
    values='Amount'        # What fills the cells
)

Critical rule: pivot() fails if any index-column combination appears more than once. It cannot aggregate — it only reshapes. In real-world data, duplicates almost always exist, so pivot() is rarely used in production.

pivot_table() — Reshape + Aggregate

df.pivot_table(
    index=['Product', 'Category'],  # Row keys
    columns='Quantity',             # What becomes columns
    values='Amount',                # Values to aggregate
    aggfunc='mean',                 # Aggregation function
    fill_value=0,                   # Replace NaN in output
    margins=False                   # Add row/column totals
)

# Multiple aggregations
df.pivot_table(
    index='Category',
    values='Amount',
    aggfunc=['mean', 'median', 'min', 'count']
)

pivot vs pivot_table

Featurepivotpivot_table
Handles duplicates❌ No✅ Yes
Aggregation❌ No✅ Yes
Production useRareVery common

Practical Example

sales = pd.DataFrame({
    'Product': ['Apple', 'Apple', 'Banana', 'Banana'],
    'Region': ['North', 'South', 'North', 'South'],
    'Revenue': [100, 150, 80, 120]
})

# Total revenue per Product per Region
pivot = sales.pivot_table(
    index='Product',
    columns='Region',
    values='Revenue',
    aggfunc='sum',
    fill_value=0
)

Key Takeaway: Think of pivot_table as groupby + aggregation + reshape combined. Use it instead of pivot in real data — it handles duplicates, supports multiple aggregations, and can add totals via margins=True.

blogs.title: Pandas Sorting, String Operations, and Data Type Conversion
blogs.description: Master Pandas sort_values() and sort_index() with all parameters, the .str accessor for vectorized string operations, and safe data type conversion with astype() and pd.to_datetime().
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
Sorting with sort_values()

df.sort_values(
    by='Age',            # Column or list of columns
    ascending=True,      # True=asc, False=desc, list=[True,False]
    inplace=False,       # Modify original
    na_position='last',  # 'first' puts NaN at top
    ignore_index=False,  # Reset index after sort
    kind='mergesort'     # Stable sort for equal elements
)

# Single column
df.sort_values('Salary', ascending=False)

# Multiple columns
df.sort_values(['Dept', 'Salary'], ascending=[True, False])

# Case-insensitive sort with key
df.sort_values('Name', key=lambda col: col.str.lower())

Sorting with sort_index()

df.sort_index()              # Sort rows by index label
df.sort_index(ascending=False)
df.sort_index(axis=1)        # Sort columns alphabetically

String Operations — The .str Accessor

The .str accessor enables vectorized string operations on a Series. It handles NaN automatically and is far faster than using .apply() with a Python function.

df['Name'].str.lower()
df['Name'].str.upper()
df['Name'].str.strip()       # Remove whitespace
df['Name'].str.len()         # Length of each string
df['Name'].str.replace('Elena', 'Emily')
df['Name'].str.contains('an', case=False, na=False)  # Returns boolean
df['Name'].str.startswith('E')
df['Name'].str.split('a')    # Returns list per row
df['Name'].str.split('a', expand=True)  # Returns DataFrame
df['Name'].str.count('a')    # Count occurrences
df['Name'].str.findall(r'[A-Z]')  # Regex match all
df['Name'].str.isnumeric()   # Check numeric string
df['Name'].str.cat(sep=', ') # Concatenate all values

Data Type Conversion

# astype() — direct conversion
df['Age'] = df['Age'].astype(int)
df['Salary'] = df['Salary'].astype(float)
df = df.astype({'Age': 'float64', 'Name': 'string'})

# errors='ignore' — leave column unchanged if conversion fails
df.astype({'A': int}, errors='ignore')

# pd.to_numeric() — safe for messy numbers
pd.to_numeric(df['Salary'], errors='coerce')  # Invalid → NaN

# pd.to_datetime() — best for date parsing
df['JoinDate'] = pd.to_datetime(df['JoinDate'], errors='coerce')
df['JoinDate'] = pd.to_datetime(df['JoinDate'], format='%d/%m/%Y')

# After converting to datetime, use .dt accessor
df['JoinDate'].dt.year
df['JoinDate'].dt.month
df['JoinDate'].dt.weekday

category Type — Memory Optimization

df['Gender'] = df['Gender'].astype('category')
# Saves memory and speeds up groupby on low-cardinality columns

Key Takeaway: Always use pd.to_datetime() for dates (not astype), and pd.to_numeric() with errors='coerce' for messy numeric columns. Use .str accessor instead of .apply(str.method) for vectorized performance.

blogs.title: Pandas I/O: Reading and Writing CSV and JSON Files
blogs.description: Complete guide to Pandas file I/O — all key parameters of read_csv() and to_csv(), handling JSON with read_json() and json_normalize() for nested data, and reading from URLs and APIs.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
pd.read_csv() — The Most Used Function

pd.read_csv(
    filepath_or_buffer,    # Path, URL, or file object
    sep=',',               # Delimiter
    header='infer',        # Row number for column names
    index_col=None,        # Column to use as index
    usecols=None,          # Load only specific columns
    nrows=None,            # Read only first N rows
    skiprows=None,         # Skip specific rows
    na_values=None,        # Additional strings to treat as NaN
    parse_dates=None,      # Columns to parse as datetime
    engine=None            # 'c' or 'python'
)

Key Parameters in Practice

# Load only needed columns (saves memory on large files)
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', usecols=['Name', 'Salary', 'Dept'])

# Set index at load time (more efficient than set_index later)
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', index_col='EmployeeID')

# Handle common missing value patterns
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', na_values=['N/A', 'NULL', 'none', ''])

# Parse dates during load
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', parse_dates=['JoinDate', 'BirthDate'])

# Handle different delimiters
df = pd.read_csv('data.tsv', sep='\t')
df = pd.read_csv('dirty.csv', sep='[,:_|]', engine='python')

# Preview large files
df = pd.read_csv('huge.csv', nrows=100)

# Skip metadata rows
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', skiprows=[0, 1, 3])

# Read from URL
df = pd.read_csv('https://example.com/data.csv')

df.to_csv() — Saving Data

import csv

df.to_csv('output.csv', index=False)               # No index column
df.to_csv('output.csv', index=False, header=False) # No header
df.to_csv('output.csv', sep='|')                   # Custom separator
df.to_csv('output.csv', na_rep='NA')               # Replace NaN
df.to_csv('output.csv', mode='a', header=False)    # Append mode
df.to_csv('output.csv', encoding='utf-8')
df.to_csv('output.csv', quoting=csv.QUOTE_ALL)     # Quote everything

JSON Handling

import json

# Read flat JSON
df = pd.read_json('data.json')

# Nested JSON — use json_normalize
with open('nested.json') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
df = pd.json_normalize(data)

# API responses
import requests
response = requests.get('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts')
df = pd.json_normalize(response.json())

# JSON Lines (one JSON per line — logs, streaming)
df = pd.read_json('events.jsonl', lines=True)

JSON Orient Options

df.to_json(orient='records')   # [{col:val}, ...] — best for APIs
df.to_json(orient='split')     # {columns, index, data} — round-trip
df.to_json(orient='index')     # {row_label: {col:val}}
df.to_json('file.json', orient='table')  # With schema metadata

Key Takeaway: Always specify usecols and parse_dates when reading large CSV files. Use json_normalize for nested JSON — pd.read_json() alone cannot handle nested structures. When appending to CSV, always pass header=False.

blogs.title: Pandas Data Cleaning: Duplicates, Normalization, and Iteration
blogs.description: Complete data cleaning workflows in Pandas — detecting and removing duplicate rows, normalizing numeric features with Min-Max and MaxAbs scaling, and choosing the right iteration method.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
Handling Duplicate Rows

Detecting Duplicates

# Returns True for rows that are duplicates
df.duplicated()                      # All columns
df.duplicated(subset=['Name'])       # Specific columns
df.duplicated(keep='last')           # Mark first as duplicate
df.duplicated(keep=False)            # Mark ALL occurrences

df.duplicated().sum()                # Count duplicates
df[df.duplicated()]                  # View duplicate rows

Removing Duplicates

df.drop_duplicates()                 # Remove exact duplicates
df.drop_duplicates(subset=['Name', 'Age'])  # By subset
df.drop_duplicates(keep='last')      # Keep last occurrence
df.drop_duplicates(keep=False)       # Remove all occurrences
df.drop_duplicates(ignore_index=True)  # Reset index after

Real-World Considerations

# Case sensitivity matters
df['Name'] = df['Name'].str.lower()

# Whitespace matters
df['Name'] = df['Name'].str.strip()

df.drop_duplicates(subset=['Name'])

Data Normalization

Normalization rescales numeric features so no single column dominates because of larger magnitude — essential before ML modeling or multi-feature comparison.

Max Absolute Scaling — Range [-1, 1]

max_scaled = df.copy()
for col in max_scaled.columns:
    max_scaled[col] = max_scaled[col] / max_scaled[col].abs().max()

Best when you want to preserve sparsity and signs (positive/negative) matter.

Min-Max Scaling — Range [0, 1]

min_max_scaled = df.copy()
for col in min_max_scaled.columns:
    min_max_scaled[col] = (
        min_max_scaled[col] - min_max_scaled[col].min()
    ) / (min_max_scaled[col].max() - min_max_scaled[col].min())

The most common normalization method, used in ML, neural networks, and distance-based algorithms. Sensitive to outliers.

Iteration — Choosing the Right Method

Pandas is built for vectorized operations, not loops. Iteration should be a last resort.

MethodSpeedUse When
Vectorized opsFastestMath, filtering, transformations
apply(axis=1)FastRow-wise custom logic
itertuples()MediumRow iteration when needed
iterrows()SlowLearning / debugging only
# Avoid this
result = []
for i, row in df.iterrows():
    result.append(row['A'] + row['B'])

# Do this instead
df['C'] = df['A'] + df['B']

# When you need row logic — use apply
df['Label'] = df.apply(
    lambda row: 'High' if row['Salary'] > 100000 else 'Low',
    axis=1
)

# When you must loop — use itertuples (fastest loop)
for row in df.itertuples():
    print(row.Name, row.Salary)

Key Takeaway: Clean before deduplication — strip whitespace and standardize case first. Always normalize only feature columns, never the target variable. Prefer vectorization over any form of loop in Pandas.

blogs.title: Pandas Index Management: set_index, reset_index, and MultiIndex
blogs.description: Everything about Pandas indexes — why they exist, set_index and reset_index with all parameters, the difference between loc and iloc with custom indexes, and an introduction to MultiIndex.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
What is an Index?

The Pandas index is the row label system — it is the identity of each row. Without it, operations like .loc, merge, join, and align would not work. Think of it as the primary key for your DataFrame rows.

# Default RangeIndex created automatically
df = pd.DataFrame({'Name': ['A', 'B'], 'Age': [25, 30]})
print(df.index)  # RangeIndex(start=0, stop=2, step=1)

# Index properties
df.index.values      # NumPy array of labels
df.index.dtype       # Data type
df.index.is_unique   # Check for duplicates

set_index() — Make a Column the Index

df.set_index(
    keys,                  # Column name or list of names
    drop=True,             # Remove column after setting
    inplace=False,
    verify_integrity=False # Error on duplicate index values
)

# Basic example
df_indexed = df.set_index('EmployeeID')
df.loc['E001']   # Now can access by ID

# Keep column AND set as index
df.set_index('Name', drop=False)

# Prevent accidental duplicate indexes
df.set_index('Email', verify_integrity=True)

reset_index() — Move Index Back to Column

df.reset_index(
    drop=False,   # Keep old index as a column (default)
    inplace=False
)

df.reset_index()         # Old index becomes a column
df.reset_index(drop=True)  # Discard old index, fresh RangeIndex

Named Index

df.index.name = 'EmployeeID'  # Give index a meaningful name

Sorting by Index

df.sort_index()              # Alphabetical / numeric sort
df.sort_index(ascending=False)

MultiIndex — Hierarchical Indexes

# Created naturally by groupby
grouped = df.groupby(['Dept', 'Team'])['Salary'].mean()
# Index is now (Dept, Team) pairs

# Access MultiIndex
grouped.loc['Engineering']           # All Engineering rows
grouped.loc[('Engineering', 'Alpha')]  # Specific group

# Reset to flat DataFrame
grouped.reset_index()

Common Patterns

# Load CSV and set index immediately (efficient)
df = pd.read_csv('employees.csv', index_col='EmployeeID')

# After groupby + aggregation, reset to clean output
agg = df.groupby('Dept').agg(avg_salary=('Salary','mean'))
agg_clean = agg.reset_index()

Key Takeaway: Index is not just a row number — it is the alignment key for all Pandas operations. Use set_index when a column uniquely identifies rows. Use verify_integrity=True in data pipelines to catch data quality issues early.

blogs.title: Pandas End-to-End: A Complete Data Analysis Workflow
blogs.description: Put it all together — a complete real-world Pandas workflow covering data loading, exploration, cleaning, transformation, aggregation, and export with best practices at each step.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
Step 1: Load and First Look

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load data efficiently
df = pd.read_csv('employees.csv',
    usecols=['Name', 'Dept', 'Salary', 'JoinDate', 'Gender'],
    parse_dates=['JoinDate'],
    na_values=['N/A', 'none', '']
)

# First inspection
print(df.shape)          # Dimensions
df.info()                # Types + null counts
df.describe()            # Statistical summary
df.head()                # First 5 rows

Step 2: Understand Data Quality

# Missing value audit
missing = df.isnull().mean() * 100
print(missing[missing > 0].sort_values(ascending=False))

# Duplicates
print(f"Duplicates: {df.duplicated().sum()}")

# Data types
print(df.dtypes)

Step 3: Clean

# Standardize text
df['Name'] = df['Name'].str.strip().str.title()
df['Dept'] = df['Dept'].str.strip().str.upper()

# Fill missing values strategically
df['Salary'] = df['Salary'].fillna(df.groupby('Dept')['Salary'].transform('median'))
df['Gender'].fillna('Unknown', inplace=True)

# Remove duplicates
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset=['Name', 'Dept'], ignore_index=True)

# Fix data types
df['Salary'] = pd.to_numeric(df['Salary'], errors='coerce')

Step 4: Transform and Engineer Features

# Derived column
df['Years_Employed'] = (pd.Timestamp.now() - df['JoinDate']).dt.days // 365

# Rank within department
df['Salary_Rank'] = df.groupby('Dept')['Salary'].transform(
    lambda x: x.rank(ascending=False, method='min')
)

# Salary tier
df['Salary_Tier'] = pd.cut(
    df['Salary'],
    bins=[0, 40000, 80000, 150000, float('inf')],
    labels=['Entry', 'Mid', 'Senior', 'Executive']
)

Step 5: Aggregate and Summarize

# Department summary
dept_summary = df.groupby('Dept').agg(
    head_count=('Name', 'count'),
    avg_salary=('Salary', 'mean'),
    max_salary=('Salary', 'max'),
    min_years=('Years_Employed', 'min')
).round(2).reset_index()

# Gender distribution per department
gender_pivot = df.pivot_table(
    index='Dept',
    columns='Gender',
    values='Name',
    aggfunc='count',
    fill_value=0
)

Step 6: Filter and Export

# Select high-performing departments
high_pay = df.loc[
    df['Salary'] > df['Salary'].quantile(0.75),
    ['Name', 'Dept', 'Salary', 'Salary_Rank']
].copy()

# Export
dept_summary.to_csv('dept_summary.csv', index=False)
high_pay.to_excel('high_performers.xlsx', index=False)

Best Practices Summary

  • Always use usecols and parse_dates when loading large files
  • Audit missing values before any transformation
  • Prefer transform() over apply() for group-aligned operations
  • Use .copy() when creating subsets you will modify
  • Chain operations but use intermediate variables for debugging
  • Use category dtype for low-cardinality string columns
  • Always reset_index after groupby for clean exportable output

Key Takeaway: A good Pandas workflow is systematic — load, inspect, clean, transform, aggregate, export. Each step has specific tools designed for it. Master these tools and 90% of data analysis problems become straightforward.
